sentences.text: Right.
We were never selected for work because, as I said, we were-- we look-- we didn't look the healthiest.
And they were looking for very healthy girls, husky, big girls, you know, that could work.
But it came-- it was in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] early September, I would say, a big selection.
Mengele came out himself with his whole crew for the selection.
We all ran outside, formed a human chain, and they started selecting again.
The barracks were empty.
The kapos-- you know, there were kapos that watched also-- didn't mention it.
They were, some of them, more vicious than the Nazis because they tried to do a very good job for the Nazis.
They maybe got a little more bread.
So they did everything that the Nazis wanted to.
I'm sure you have heard.
And there was a lot that the-- that some of the kapos, when they came back, people were really after them.
Well, anyhow, the barrack was empty, and there was the big selection.
Those that were-- we were lined up, and they were pulling them, left-- again, left, right.
Those that were selected for work were put into the barracks.
And those not were human chain.
Well, we already had a feeling, if you're not selected for labor, then you're only good for the oven.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So a whole bunch of us, after Mengele has left with his Nazis, we pushed ourselves through the, that human chain and ran into the barracks, started running.
The kapos were standing by the door, but we were-- there were a lot of us.
We overpowered them and were able to get into the barrack.
But they beat the dickens out of us, over the head, over the faces, over our whole body, you know, beating us.
They, they were-- they-- their weapon was a, a rubber-- what do you call it?
The police carry them.
The sticks.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So they were beating us, but nevertheless, we got into the barrack.
And then, you know, we're mixed already.
You don't know who it was.
By the way, sorry to, to go back.
My sister has been selected several times for the oven.
I was tall.
My sister is smaller than I am.
She was only 16 years old.
She looks like a child of 12.
So she was selected, but as I say, she was always aggressive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She ran back into the line with some of the others.
Once she ran back, and the Nazi came after her.
She went one row-- you know, we were standing rows of five girls-- behind her.
He pulled out one sister.
There were five sisters together.
Pulled out the youngest.
She was younger than my sister.
They pulled her out.
So gave the first a good beating.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And somebody else went instead of my sister.
But she was always very fast.
Well, anyhow, we got in, into the barracks and-- after the beating and all that.
And then they took us to a train that they're going to send us in different places, working places in Germany.
But I guess it must have been, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it must have been bombed, the railways, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that they turned us around and send us back.
Back to Auschwitz in a different camp, though, not in the same, not in the same camp that we were in, in another camp.
And we stayed there for 24 hours.
And then one day, they said, line up, all of you.
And they started giving the tattoos out, putting the, the numbers on it.
And then, I guess, that they ran behind or something.
They didn't have a chance to do it on us.
And this is how they took us.
They lined up.
And they said, 500 girls in these and these cattle cars.
And I don't know how many in others.
So we were 500 girls were selected to go.
None of us had the numbers.
Because it was already fast-- because I believe October, Auschwitz was liberated.
So they tried to get out as many as possible, either to work or to the ovens, one way or another.
So we were in the trains stopping and standing and going.
But nevertheless, the idea was that we were going to a working camp, which was already a little more hope.
So they brought us to-- the, the city was-- we were told later it was Lubberstedt in Germany.
It was in the woods.
There were barracks.
We were, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were about 36 girls in one room.
And by coincidence, about 30 of us were from my hometown.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In there, there we had already everyone bunks, double bunks, no mattresses, just a little in the wood, but it was a bunk, nevertheless.
And it was a blanket was given to the individual so we were able-- it was more comfortable.
It was in the woods.
And then they started taking girls that would work in the kitchen or in the warehouses.
And they gave us also clothes.
The striped uniforms, they gave us.
And they gave us coats.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The coat, you had to take out the sleeve, one sleeve, and change it somebody else to put in another sleeve in order should recognize that these are prisoners, or whatever.
And then on top of that, one had to paint for the other a big yellow paint on the back of the coat.
It should be visible from distance that these are prisoners.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So as I say, we were not selected for the kitchen or the warehouse or anything but to work.
So the next day-- again, we didn't have anything, but we-- yeah, we had the Dutch sandals.
Dutch shoes we had.
They gave us.
So this was also-- which here we didn't have anything.
Some were barefoot before in Auschwitz.
Some, some had one shoe like this, one shoe like that, you know.
It wasn't pairs.
But here we had the Dutch slippers.
And so they took us to a, a, a factory where they were manufacturing bombs that they throw from the airplanes.
And we were working there.
There were also-- Germans were working there, and Ukrainians were also working there.
And everybody was assigned to different departments, temporary.
My job was either stenciling on the bomb, the number, or-- since I looked like a ghost, they didn't give me a hard job.
We had some-- unfortunately, some girls from, from-- they were working with the, with the bombs with the, with the raw material, and their hair has turned red.
They contracted tuberculosis.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wasn't one of those, neither was my sister.
And so we were working every, every morning.
Get up at 5 o'clock in the morning.
They gave us-- the food was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] coffee.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And everybody had a bowl.
We didn't have to share like we did in Auschwitz, 10, 10 people wanting soup.
Everybody had an individual bowl.
And they were counting us in the morning, of course.
And then they gave us the soup.
And they give us a little soup for lunch also.
The van came out to the factory, and they gave us for dinner a little soup with bread.
So you could never try to keep a piece of bread for the morning coffee because you were so starved.
So never able-- couldn't sleep until I knew that there was still a bite of bread left.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So I worked in the, the factory, the bomb factory there for months.
Was bitter, bitter cold, marching in the snow in those wooden things.
And if you didn't keep up the march once or twice a day-- I mean, the one day or the second day, they pulled you out, and they finished you off.
And got adjusted to it.
Life was, life was going on.
We have even accomplished that we knew approximately when Yom Kippur was, that they wouldn't serve us the food, but they'll give us in the evening the food.
We marched to work.
We went to work.
The women, the older women sort of figured it out.
And I think one Nazi also let it out, yes, it is Yom Kippur.
Because they knew, so that they didn't serve us food.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how we conduct Yom Kippur Eve in those barracks-- some of the girls, it was terrible.
Where is God?
Didn't answer us.
So in the morning, we went to work, and it passed.
And then when it came the really, late already the winter, they didn't have enough gas for the trucks.
The trucks-- we would load the, the bombs on the truck and the truck would take them into a-- underground bunkers, they called them.
But then they, they ran out of gas.
They didn't have any gas.
I think that was during the war for the Nazis a minus.
They didn't have enough gas, gasoline.
So they took us, the girls, and we were pulling those trucks to the bunkers in the big snow.
Sometimes the shoe would get stuck in the big snow.
So we've got to work, move.
And then it was stopped altogether.
They didn't have, I guess, material, whatever.
But we would, they would-- there would be a lot of air raids-- this is how we figured it, already-- a lot of air raids, almost nightly.

We would be woken up and taken into the bunkers, hidden because they were not afraid for us that we were going to perish or something, but they were afraid, you know, to put on the lights and then planes might spot it.
So sometimes they-- for that reason, they kept us in those bunkers with food and water.
And one night, tragedy happened to me.
Somebody-- I fell asleep because we were so tired from the marching to work, marching from work, and pulling those things that my wooden shoe, somebody took it off of my foot.
And I was left barefoot in the morning.
I have to march, and they'll kill me if I show up without it.
What am I going to do?

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a cousin that worked in the warehouse, so she jeopardized her life-- she wasn't supposed to-- and ran early in the morning and asked one of the Nazis to open it up, because she said she forgot something there and she needed it badly.
So she stole a, a pair of wooden clogs and brought it for me.
Otherwise they would have killed me there without having them.
Once, once it was bitter cold, so I took a little piece of a blanket, and I tied it around me.
Skinny, I was terrible.
I tied it around because I was bitter cold.
Then a Nazi pulled me out, and she spotted on me the blanket.
She beat the daylight out of me, because I-- government property I, I had taken out from the barrack.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I knew for the next time.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But life was going pretty smoothly until they stopped manufacturing the bombs.
Then it was already closer to spring.
No, it was still winter, yeah.
Then they started-- they took us, and they started digging ditches.
And that's where I-- my hands were frozen.
My legs and my toes got frozen.
And I suffered after liberation.
After the thing I suffered a lot.
Every year it would get terribly infected.
And it was something that I didn't want to go to a doctor, and I didn't want to talk about it, even then.
Things like that, that I refused to go see a doctor, but I suffered.
My hands were-- ran with pus and all that, very infected.
Still have marks.
But it healed up after several years.
It healed up.
After digging the ditches, then in the spring, they started selecting girls to work for the farmers in the fields.
I was never among the lucky ones.
At least they got fed there.
You know, from work we were not afraid, but for hunger was the-- hunger was the most horrible, horrible thing.
They got fed there.
The farmer fed them.
So the girls would say that they looked so bewildered.
They put out the food, and they-- the girls start grabbing it and putting it into their bosoms and-- they just couldn't understand that they'll bring you more.
Don't grab from the table.
So the next day, they would send another group.
I was never-- we were never-- not my sister, not me, were ever lucky to be selected.
But here and there there was a Nazi with humanity in him.
Like she would look at my sister.
She was so small and fragile [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that she would call her over and give her a little bit leftover of her food, shared with me.
So-- and then one day they just announced for us that they had, from the Red Cross, they received orders.
They're going to take us to Sweden on a boat, and we're all going to be liberated.
So the happiness was beyond words.

They told us, all you can take is-- we didn't have anything what to take-- but whoever wants to take their bowl, you know, for in case somewhere somebody is going to be kind enough to serve us some food.
We took the bowl with us.
Again, they put us into cattle cars.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were travelling.
It was-- we put in-- we were 500 girls.
And they put us in a very, very long train with ammunitions tanks and all kinds of ammunitions.
The, the train-- and we were 500, and those cattle cars, it was miserable, just miserable, because they didn't give us any food.
There was no toilet facilities.
Crammed together, terribly hungry.
You didn't move your legs, so you were paralyzed.
You couldn't move your legs.
So one day they asked us-- they stopped, and they were going for water to serve us water-- for volunteers.
So I was-- I always volunteered.
I was never-- so he took me, the Nazi, for water, just to, to stretch your legs.
And there were some Slavic prisoners of war.
And I understood the Slavic language.
So he asked me in Slavic whether I'm hungry.
And I said, yeah.
While I was pumping the water from the pump, they ran out, and he put something warm-- it felt like he did a fried potato or something like it-- put in my pocket.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the Nazi noticed it.
He beat me mercilessly.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I thought that he broke my hip with his boots.
With his boot, he was kicking me, like a dog.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My sister [INAUDIBLE] was scared when I came back without water because he wouldn't let me already bring the water to punish me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Survived that also.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But after a while, [SIGHS] they started bombing the train.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They came.
We could hear the planes coming down low and smashing it.
Could see the, the cattle car that we were on fell-- bomb fell on that, too.
My sister was the first one.
She was by the door.
And the Nazis only opened up for one person to be able to get out.
They didn't open up the whole door, because they actually didn't want us to go out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So she was the first one to be pushed out.
And this cattle car was very high.
She got herself all beaten up, jumping.
In the field, you could see there were cattle in the pastures.
And I thought to myself, this is it.
No more, no more.
I'm going to stay.
I'm not running.
They started pushing.
And I says, I'm not going to jump.
I want to be killed right here already, among here, already.
Many of them that were killed died in the, in the cattle car.
But she was so hysterical, standing there outside for me that people pushed me out.
They grabbed me and pushed me out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the bombs were falling all over.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Lost a lot of girls at that time, maybe half, maybe a little less.
But it was a peculiar thing, as if God had it that way.
Mothers and daughters, sisters together, perished together.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, anyhow, after the bombing, after quite a while, the bombing had stopped.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Nazis were kind enough that for the wounded, for the badly wounded, they gave an extra piece of bread.
We were so starved already that we were grabbing it from those, from those that couldn't handle it anymore anyhow.
Half bloody, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the piece of bread.
We ate it.
And it became evening.
It got dark.
They lined us up again, back in the, in the cattle cars.
Somehow the, the locomotive or whatever wasn't damaged, and they were able to move.
And they moved us over the dead bodies.
They didn't take them out.
There were all the dead bodies.
Didn't matter.
You were waiting already.
You knew that time is getting closer, that this is where you'll wind up anyhow.
So it's-- you know, you didn't mourn the dead.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So we were going still maybe for a day or so.
And then again the rail, must have been bombed, but they got us off.
Hungry, cold, weak, worn out, they marched us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But they became a little bit more lenient.
Like we saw fields where there was-- you know, farmers would have, you know, in the winter, have, like, dug holes and put potatoes or, or, you know, different vegetables that can sustain the cold.
So we saw those things.
So we asked them if we can go and grab something.
And they allowed us, just for a couple minutes, go and grab a potato, a raw potato, or a carrot.
Some were lucky.
They had a carrot.
So after the marching, they just abandoned us.
And one Nazi, she was-- she said herself she knew everything about Jewish life.
She [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] must have been a maid in a Jewish family, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but vicious, that they don't come worse.
She was very small, very ugly.
But a beating-- what she could do.
I guess the bitterness she would let out on us, on all of us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So she sort of said, yeah, they told us, but then before it was that we're taking us to Sweden on a boat.
She says, they're not going.
They're want to kill you all here.
She says, you-- those that want to run, run.
Run for your life.
Now, the next day, it didn't take long.
This must have been around the 3rd of May or something. 1945.
Because a couple of days later, the British had marched in, so we were just figuring from there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We didn't run, because there was no place to run.
And the, the Germans were all over with guns that if you-- they saw you, you looked like a prisoner or a mental person.
First you wore that striped uniform and that, that coat that you wore with the, with the yellow and the things.
It was-- they saw that you were either a mental, from a mental institution or a prisoner somewhere.
They lined us up again, and they started pushing us into the cattle cars.
And the, the wonderful, wonderful Germans came to help from all around, running.
There is one hiding.
There is one hiding.
Go get her.
Or they would go themselves and pull you and bring you.
But they didn't have an opportunity because there must have been orders to run so that they left us.
They didn't take us any further.
And--
They-- we were-- we jumped.
We were able to go-- get off already from the train because it wasn't locked.
But there were no Nazis around, so we knew that something is going to happen soon.
So we stayed.
We were hiding in the woods also.
I remember there was a farmer that had a big estate.
And he was feeding his chickens with bread.
So we ran over to grab a little piece of that bread.
So he came out with a gun and started cussing.
And he says, if you don't leave, I'll kill you all here.
So we didn't.
But then we were sort of walking in daze, looking whether you can pick up a little food.
And we came to the railroad station where the, those cattle cars were.
And there were refugees from-- you know, Gentiles from Slavic or whatever, prisoners or whatever.
They had broken open some of the cattle cars.
And there was food.
So I got two breads.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] While I was getting the two breads, I was attacked by others.
Hungry people grabbed those two breads from me.
And went back.
I was able to get a piece of frozen raw fish.
We ate it.
We got very sick because, first of all, our stomachs were very-- you know, we didn't have food.
